feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: Fraser Burns — Quality Team Leader, engineering enthusiast, and lifelong learner. Showcasing professional experience, projects, and writing on quality management and making.

entry.title: Where I Am Going and Why It Matters

link.title: Where I Am Going and Why It Matters

entry.content:
I am a Quality Team Leader. That is where I am right now, and on most days it is where my energy goes: maintaining and updating the QMS, making sure the team has what they need, working to build a culture where quality is not an afterthought but part of how things are done here.

It is good work. But it is not the destination.

Where I Want to Get To

The goal is Quality Manager. Not because of the title, but because of what it means in practice: the ability to shape how an organisation approaches quality at a level where it actually sticks. To influence processes, culture, and standards in a way that makes a real difference to how people work and what customers receive.

That is what I am working toward.

The Roadmap

I am deliberate about how I get there. These are the qualifications I am working through and why each one matters:

HNC Mechanical Engineering (in progress) - the technical foundation. Understanding the engineering context behind the quality work I do every day.

Lead Auditor - learning to assess quality systems from the outside in. Auditing disciplines your thinking and sharpens your ability to identify where a system is strong and where it is not.

ASQ Certified Quality Manager - a recognised professional standard from one of the most respected quality bodies in the world. This is about demonstrating that the knowledge is real and tested, not just accumulated on the job.

ASQ Quality Engineer - deepening the technical side. Quality engineering sits at the intersection of process, data, and improvement, and I want to be competent across all of it.

CQI Chartered Status - the professional benchmark here in the UK. Chartered membership of the Chartered Quality Institute represents a commitment to the profession and to continuous development within it.

None of these are boxes to tick. Each one represents something I want to understand better and be able to apply.

Why It Matters

Quality done well changes how organisations work. It is not just about catching defects or closing non-conformances. It is about building systems and cultures where problems are prevented in the first place, where people have the clarity and support to do their jobs properly, and where the standard is maintained because everyone understands why it matters.

That is worth committing to. It is the kind of professional I want to be.

I have seen what a well-run quality function looks like and what it does for the people working within it. I have also seen the opposite. The difference is significant, and it does not happen by accident. It takes people who are genuinely invested in getting it right.

The Path Is Long

I am not going to pretend this happens quickly. The qualifications take time, the experience has to be earned, and building something worth building at Scott & Fyfe is itself an ongoing project.

But the direction is set. The reasons are clear. That is enough to keep going.
entry.summary: A reflection on where I am in my quality career, where I am headed, and the qualifications and reasons driving that direction.

entry.title: Inspector To Team Leader
entry.content:
i— layout: post title: “From Inspector to Team Leader: What Nobody Tells You” date: 2026-03-29 09:00:00 description: Reflections on moving from Quality Control Inspector to Quality Team Leader - the mindset shift, the surprises, and what I would tell myself before making the move. tags: quality management career categories: quality-management —

There is a moment I keep coming back to from my early days as a Quality Team Leader. A non-conformance had been raised on the shop floor and my first instinct was to get up, walk over, and sort it out myself. I knew exactly what to look for. I had done it hundreds of times. It would have taken me ten minutes.

I sat back down.

Not because I was lazy or disinterested, but because I had just realised something: that instinct, the one that had served me well for over a decade as an inspector, was now the wrong instinct. My job was no longer to find and fix problems. It was to build a team and a system that could do that without me.

That shift sounds simple. It is not.

From the Shop Floor to the System

When you work as an inspector, you live on the shop floor. The feedback loop is short and clear. Something arrives, you assess it, you pass or fail it. You know where you stand.

Moving into a team leader role means stepping back from that world and working on the system behind it. You are no longer the one looking at the part. You are responsible for whether the right processes, standards, and training are in place so that quality is built in from the start. The feedback loop gets longer. The results are less visible. You do good work today and you might not see the outcome for months. You might not see it at all, because the problem you prevented never appeared.

That transition from shop floor thinking to systems thinking was a challenge when I made the move. Honestly, it still is. There are days when the pull to just go and sort something out yourself is strong. But that is not the job anymore.

The Manager I Wanted to Be

Across my career in quality, I have worked in environments where the team had real support: clear goals, documented processes, open communication, and a culture where you could raise your hand and ask a question without hesitation. That kind of environment makes a difference. You know what good looks like, you have the tools to achieve it, and you feel backed when things get difficult.

That is the benchmark I carry with me. It shaped what I want to build.

At Scott & Fyfe, the goal is straightforward even if the work is not: a quality team with a robust QMS underpinning everything they do, and a culture where people feel supported enough to flag problems early, ask questions freely, and take ownership of the standard. Clear direction. Solid processes. No guessing about what is expected.

What the Role Actually Demands

Getting there requires things that inspection did not prepare me for.

Trusting the team is harder than it sounds when you have spent years developing a sharp eye for defects. The instinct is to step in. But stepping in too often builds dependence, not capability. The job is to develop the team’s judgement, not replace it.

Communicating upward is a different skill entirely. Quality problems need to be translated into language that lands with people who are not living in the detail: what is the risk, what is the cost, what needs to happen. That translation is part of the role now.

And the credibility that comes from having done the job yourself matters more than I expected. The team knows you understand what you are asking of them. That earns trust in a way that a job title alone never could.

What Carried Over

The inspector’s habit of asking “why” until you reach the actual cause - not the surface answer, but the real one - is just as useful in a leadership role. Most quality problems have a process or system failure underneath them. That instinct to keep digging is worth keeping.

The eye for detail shifted too. You use it less to inspect individual parts and more to notice when something in the team or the system is not quite right. A process that has drifted. A team member who seems uncertain. A standard that is not being followed consistently.

Still Building

I will not pretend the transition is complete or that what I am working toward is fully in place. Moving from inspector to team leader is a work in progress, and some days the gap between the vision and the reality is more obvious than others.

But the direction is clear. Build the system. Support the team. Be the kind of leader that makes the work easier, not harder. That is worth the challenge.
entry.summary: i— layout: post title: “From Inspector to Team Leader: What Nobody Tells You” date: 2026-03-29 09:00:00 description: Reflections on moving from Quality Control Inspector to Quality Team Leader - the mindset shift, the surprises, and what I would tell myself before making the move. tags: quality management career categories: quality-management —
